g.data-narration: TCP/IP Protocol Suite is the foundation of modern networking, powering the Internet and LANs worldwide.
TCP/IP was developed by Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn in the 1970s-80s, becoming the global communication standard.
g.text: A set of network protocols for interconnecting devices across diverse networks.
Provides end-to-end communication: packetizing, addressing, transmitting, routing,
and receiving data with robust, flexible, and scalable architecture.

g.data-narration: TCP/IP has 4 practical layers while OSI is a 7-layer conceptual framework. TCP/IP is the implementation standard.
The TCP/IP model consists of Network Access, Internet, Transport, and Application layers, each with specific protocols.
The Network Access Layer handles physical transmission, framing, MAC addressing, and controls access to the transmission medium.
g.text: • Handles physical transmission of data over network medium
• Functions: Framing data, Physical addressing (MAC), Medium access control

g.data-narration: The Internet Layer handles logical addressing with IP addresses and routing packets across networks using IP, ICMP, ARP protocols.
Transport Layer has TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) and UDP (unreliable, connectionless). TCP ensures delivery with flow control.
g.text: • Used by: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP
Applications: Video calls, Gaming, Live streaming

g.data-narration: Application Layer interfaces with user applications using HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH for various network services.
IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (~4.3 billion) while IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses providing virtually unlimited space.
IP addresses are classified: Class A (large networks), B (medium), C (small), D (multicast), E (reserved).
TCP establishes connection via SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK handshake. This ensures both ends are ready for data transfer.
Port numbers (0-65535) identify applications: Well-known (0-1023), Registered (1024-49151), Dynamic (49152-65535).
g.text: HTTP:80 | HTTPS:443 | FTP:21 | SSH:22 | SMTP:25 | DNS:53

g.data-narration: Data encapsulation adds headers at each layer: Application → Transport → Internet → Network Access. Reverse is de-encapsulation.

g.text: Subnetting formula: Usable hosts = 2^(32-n) - 2 where n = CIDR prefix

g.data-narration: Remember: TCP/IP has 4 layers (not 7 like OSI). TCP is reliable, UDP is unreliable. Well-known ports: 80, 443, 21, 22, 25, 53.
g.text: HTTP:80 | HTTPS:443 | FTP:21 | SSH:22 | SMTP:25 | DNS:53

g.data-narration: TCP/IP is the backbone of modern networking with 4 layers: Application, Transport, Internet, and Network Access protocols.
g.text: TCP/IP: 4 Layers | TCP=Reliable, UDP=Fast | IPv4(32-bit), IPv6(128-bit) | Subnetting for network division
Key: HTTP(80), HTTPS(443), FTP(21), SSH(22), SMTP(25), DNS(53) | Private IPs: 10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x
